title: The three genders — masculine, feminine, neuter

content_json.content: In German, every noun is one of three genders. The gender is shown by the definite article before the noun. Unlike biological gender, grammatical gender in German is not always logical — a girl (das Mädchen) is neuter, a key (der Schlüssel) is masculine. You must learn the article with every noun.

cards.label: MASCULINE
cards.examples: der Mann · der Tisch · der Hund · der Januar

cards.label: FEMININE
cards.examples: die Frau · die Schule · die Stadt · die Zeit

cards.label: NEUTER
cards.examples: das Kind · das Haus · das Buch · das Auto

content_json.content: Why gender matters beyond the article: Gender affects every part of the sentence around the noun. When you say "I see a man" — the article for "man" changes from der to den (Akkusativ). When you say "I give the woman" — the article changes from die to der (Dativ). You cannot learn cases without knowing gender first. Every wrong gender creates a chain of wrong forms.
The indefinite article (a/an) also changes by gender

Masculine and neuter share "ein" — only feminine gets "eine." This matters in writing but not in speech (they sound different in sentences). The distinction between ein and eine is the first gender test in Goethe Schreiben form-filling.
Hear the articles — tap each
Learn the noun and article as one unit: der for masculine, die for feminine, das for neuter. Use ein with masculine and neuter nouns, and eine with feminine nouns.

title: Gender rules — patterns you can use right now

content_json.content: You cannot memorise the gender of every German noun. But you can learn the patterns that predict gender most of the time. These rules cover the majority of the vocabulary in Goethe A1.
Rules for der — masculine

content_json.title: Male people and male animals
content_json.examples: der Vater (father) · der Bruder (brother) · der Hund (male dog) · der Lehrer (male teacher)
content_json.description: Any noun referring to a male person or male animal is grammatically masculine.

content_json.title: Days, months, seasons
content_json.examples: der Montag · der Januar · der Sommer · der Herbst
content_json.description: You learned this in Module 1 — all three categories are masculine.

content_json.title: Endings: -er, -en, -el, -ling, -ig, -ismus
content_json.examples: der Lehrer (-er) · der Wagen (-en) · der Schlüssel (-el) · der Frühling (-ling)
content_json.description: Nouns ending in these suffixes are usually masculine.

content_json.content: Rules for die — feminine

content_json.title: Female people and female animals
content_json.examples: die Mutter (mother) · die Schwester (sister) · die Ärztin (female doctor)
content_json.description: Any noun referring to a female person or female animal is grammatically feminine.

content_json.title: Endings: -ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft, -ion, -ik, -tät, -enz
content_json.examples: die Wohnung (-ung) · die Freiheit (-heit) · die Möglichkeit (-keit) · die Nation (-ion) · die Universität (-tät)
content_json.description: These endings are almost always feminine. If a noun ends in -ung, it is die — no exceptions.

content_json.content: Rules for das — neuter

content_json.title: Endings: -chen, -lein, -ment, -um, -tum
content_json.examples: das Mädchen (girl) · das Häuschen (little house) · das Argument (-ment) · das Zentrum (-um)
content_json.description: Diminutives ending in -chen or -lein are always neuter — even if they refer to a female person (das Mädchen = the girl).

content_json.title: Most countries and continents
content_json.examples: das Europa · das Afrika · but: die Türkei · die Schweiz
content_json.description: Most country names have no article (in Kenia, in Deutschland). But the few that do take an article are neuter: das Deutschland (only used in "das schöne Deutschland"). Exceptions: die Türkei, die Schweiz, die USA (plural).

content_json.content: Do not choose gender only from a noun’s meaning. First look for a reliable ending, then learn the article with the word.
Start with the strongest patterns: male people and time words are often der; -ung, -heit, and -keit signal die; -chen and -lein signal das.

title: Home and daily life — the nouns Goethe tests most

content_json.content: These are the nouns that appear most frequently in Goethe A1 Hören and Lesen. Tap any card to hear the German.
Home and Living
Food and Daily Life
Do not memorise "Tisch" or "Wohnung" alone. The article is part of the word: der Tisch, die Wohnung.
Say each article with the noun, then picture its colour: blue for der, red for die, and green for das.

title: Work, people, and places — tap to hear each

content_json.content: Learn people, professions, and places as complete article+noun pairs.
Family and People

das Mädchen — the most famous gender trap: "Girl" is grammatically neuter in German because it ends in -chen (diminutive). The biology says female; the grammar says neuter. The grammar wins. Always: das Mädchen, es ist... (not sie ist in a grammar context).
Professions — pairs
Places and Transport
Professions often use der for the masculine form and die for the feminine -in form. Keep learning every place and transport noun with its article.

title: Plural forms — all plurals use die

content_json.content: Every German noun, regardless of its singular gender, uses die in the plural. der Tisch → die Tische. das Kind → die Kinder. die Frau → die Frauen. The article is always die in the plural. The hard part is that the noun itself changes — there are several plural patterns. You must learn the plural form of each noun along with its gender.
Immediate example: der Tisch → die Tische.
The plural patterns

lines.text: Ich habe zwei Kinder. (I have two children — Kinder = plural of Kind)
Die Züge fahren um acht Uhr ab. (The trains depart at eight — Züge = plural of Zug)
lines.audio: Die Züge fahren um acht Uhr ab.

lines.text: Die Ärzte sind im Krankenhaus. (The doctors are in the hospital — Ärzte = plural of Arzt)
Wir haben drei Zimmer in der Wohnung. (We have three rooms — Zimmer = no change in plural)
lines.audio: Wir haben drei Zimmer in der Wohnung.

content_json.content: The plural article is always die, but the noun ending is not always the same. Learn the complete plural form.
Every plural uses die. Learn each noun as article + singular + plural because the noun ending may change.

Use the article before a noun as a clue to its gender and meaning. The Goethe exam tests gender in several ways: form-filling (writing the correct article), Lesen (choosing the right sentence), and Schreiben (producing sentences). These are the patterns that appear most often.
In the Goethe Lesen section, the article narrows down what the noun can be. "Der" signals a masculine noun. If you hear "die" before a time word, you know it is die Zeit, die Stunde, or die Uhrzeit.
Typical Goethe A1 form fields

rows: feminine — ends in -e (many -e nouns are feminine)

lines.text: Der Kurs beginnt am Montag. (The course begins on Monday — der Kurs, masculine)
Die Öffnungszeit ist von neun bis achtzehn Uhr. (The opening time is from nine to six — die Öffnungszeit, feminine)
lines.audio: Die Öffnungszeit ist von neun bis achtzehn Uhr.

lines.text: Das Formular muss vollständig ausgefüllt werden. (The form must be filled out completely — das Formular, neuter)
lines.audio: Das Formular muss vollständig ausgefüllt werden.

lines.text: Die Wohnung hat drei Zimmer und eine Küche. (The apartment has three rooms and a kitchen — die Wohnung, feminine)
lines.audio: Die Wohnung hat drei Zimmer und eine Küche.

content_json.content: Do not ignore a short article while listening. It may be the fastest clue to the noun you heard.
In forms, reading, and listening, use the article to identify the noun’s gender and narrow down its meaning.
